issues.description: Pedagogically dangerous framing: 'dy/dx is not a fraction — it is a single symbol — but it behaves like one in many contexts.' This contradicts later usage (differentials, separation of variables in later units, chain rule du/dx · dx/dt cancellations) without giving the student any justification. AP students who internalize 'it's just a symbol' often miss free-response points where Leibniz manipulation is expected.
issues.fix: Reword to: 'dy/dx is the limit of Δy/Δx, so it is not literally a fraction of two numbers — but because it is the limit of a ratio, it inherits fraction-like behavior (chain rule, differentials) that we will justify rigorously when we meet each rule.' This is honest and doesn't set up a contradiction.

issues.description: The alternate (point-form) limit definition f'(a) = lim(x→a) [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a) is not introduced anywhere. AP FRQs (especially differentiability/piecewise problems) frequently rely on this form, and CHA 2.1/2.2 explicitly lists both forms.
issues.fix: Add a one-line callout on slide 05: 'Equivalent form: f'(a) = lim(x→a) [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a) — useful when you want to plug in x = a directly.' Or add a small dedicated slide.

issues.description: The symmetric difference quotient is stated correctly numerically, but the slide should name it and note that it generally gives a better estimate than the forward or backward difference quotient — that pedagogical point is what makes it AP-worthy. Also, no mention of units (e.g., 'if f is position in meters and x is seconds, f'(2) is in m/s'), which AP graders love to see.
issues.fix: Add: 'This is the symmetric difference quotient; it averages forward and backward slopes and is usually more accurate than either alone.' Add a one-line units reminder: 'Units of f'(a) = (units of f) / (units of x).'

issues.description: Only the corner (|x| at 0) failure mode is shown. AP also tests vertical-tangent failure (e.g., x^(1/3) at 0) and jump/cusp failures. Listing only corners can leave students thinking 'no corner = differentiable.'
issues.fix: Add a small inset: 'Three ways differentiability can fail: corner (|x|), cusp (x^(2/3)), vertical tangent (x^(1/3)), and any discontinuity.'

issues.description: d/dx[1/x] = −x^(−2) is correct but stylistically the equivalent −1/x^2 should appear since AP solutions and rubrics typically use the positive-exponent form. Also worth a brief note that the power rule is being stated for all real n (not just positive integers), which is the AP-level claim.
issues.fix: Show '−x^(−2) = −1/x^2' and add a parenthetical: '(holds for all real n; we'll see why in later units).'

issues.description: The worked example d/dx[sin x / x] = (cos x · x − sin x · 1) / x^2 is correct but should be simplified to (x cos x − sin x) / x^2, which is the form a rubric would accept. Leaving it unsimplified models sloppy AP work.
issues.fix: Add the simplified final form: '= (x cos x − sin x) / x^2.'

issues.description: All four derivatives are correct. The mnemonic 'co-functions get negatives' is fine but it would help to also note that cot and csc both have negative derivatives AND that sec/csc derivatives contain a product of two trig functions (sec·tan, csc·cot) — a common source of student blanks.
issues.fix: Add a two-line pattern note: 'sec' and csc' are products of two trig functions; cot' and csc' carry the minus sign.'

issues.description: Worked answer d/dx[x · cos x] = 1·cos x + x·(−sin x) = cos x − x sin x is computed correctly. Pedagogically, it would be worth explicitly identifying which factor is f and which is g before applying the rule, mirroring slide 11's template.
issues.fix: Show 'f = x, f' = 1; g = cos x, g' = −sin x' as a labeled box before the substitution, matching the slide 11 template exactly.

issues.description: No mention anywhere of the equation of the tangent line y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a), which is a CHA 2.2-adjacent skill the AP exam tests in nearly every FRQ from Unit 2 onward. Easy add and high-leverage.
issues.fix: Add one bullet to slide 05 or the recap: 'Tangent line at x = a: y = f(a) + f'(a)(x − a).'

overall_comment: Formulas and worked computations are all correct (power rule, product rule, quotient rule with sin x / x, x · cos x product rule, and all six trig derivatives verified). The two items worth flagging hard are the 'dy/dx is not a fraction' framing (will create a misconception when separation-of-variables shows up) and the missing point-form limit definition f'(a) = lim(x→a)(f(x)−f(a))/(x−a), which the AP CED lists as co-equal with the h-form. Everything else is polish.
